text:
"White Fang" follows a part-dog, part-wolf creature named White Fang, introducing the harsh realities of survival in the wilds of North America. Born in a den in the Indian territory of Yukon, White Fang's life is a constant struggle from the moment he opens his eyes. He learns survival from his mother, Kiche, who fights to protect him from the other dogs in their camp, as well as the cruel elements of the wild. The novel vividly describes White Fang's early experiences of nurturing and brutality, influencing his later outlook on life and humans. 

As a cub, he is captured by an Indian tribe, recognizing one of the tribal members, Grey Beaver, as his master. While living in the tribe, White Fang learns to obey, respect, and fear the “law of the fang,” a concept that only the toughest, most ruthless dogs survive. Despite his loyalty to Grey Beaver, he is constantly provoked and attacked by the other dogs, pushing his survival instinct to the limit.

The story takes a grim turn when White Fang is sold to a cruel man named Beauty Smith. He forces White Fang to take part in inhumane dog fights, intensifying his harsh outlook on life. The wolf-dog's life changes dramatically when he is rescued by a compassionate man, Weedon Scott. Scott's kindness and love gradually erase years of brutality, instilling trust in humans in White Fang's heart. The novel concludes showcasing the power of love and kindness to rehabilitate and heal, as White Fang enjoys a peaceful life with Scott in California, a stark contrast to his previously tumultuous existence in the icy wilderness.

3 1/2 ounces flour
A pinch of salt
1/2 ounce sugar
A pinch of baking powder
2 eggs
1 ounce butter
1 quart water
18 ounces caster sugar
Lemon zests
Orange zests
2 ounces of apricot coating/glazing
Add Rum according to your taste
7 ounces double cream
1 ounce sugar
1/2 vanilla pod
Mix all the ingredients without the butter and then beat with a wooden spoon for about 15 minutes. When the dough starts to unstick from the spoon, add the melted butter. Mix. Cover the dough with a cloth and let it rise. When the volume doubles spread the dough halfway up the 4 buttered moulds. Make sure that the dough is evenly spread and than leave it to rise in a warm area (86 - 95F). The Baba should slightly overflow the moulds. Cook it in the oven for 25 minutes at 392 - 400F. The cooking time can depend on the size, colour, and desired moistness of the Baba. Turn out the Babas as soon as they are cook. Mix all the syrup ingredients and bring to boiling point. Leave to infuse. Place the Babas in the syrup and leave to soak for about 40 minutes. Verify that they are well soaked, and then place them on a grid to let them drip for about 5 minutes. Slice the vanilla pod in two lengthwise and scrape out the grains that will be mixed in with the cream. Beat the vanilla grains with the sugar and cream to obtain a Chantilly. Put the Baba on a plate and glaze with the apricot coating. Serving at the table, cut the Baba in two (lengthwise) and pour on the rum according to you taste. Served it with the Chantilly cream.

Many practical sensing applications involve multiple sensors simultaneously
acquiring measurements of a single object. Conversely, most existing sparse
recovery guarantees in compressed sensing concern only single-sensor
acquisition scenarios. In this paper, we address optimal recovery of
compressible signals from multi-sensor measurements using compressed sensing
techniques, thereby confirming benefits of multi- over single-sensor
environments. Throughout paper, we consider a broad class of sensing
matrices, and two fundamentally different sampling scenarios (distinct and
identical respectively), both of which are relevant to applications. For the
case of diagonal sensor profile matrices (which characterize environmental
conditions between source and the sensors), this paper presents two key
improvements over existing results. First, a simpler optimal recovery guarantee
for distinct sampling, and second, improved recovery guarantee for identical
sampling, based on the so-called sparsity in levels signal model.

"Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell: Operation Barracuda" follows the story of Sam Fisher, an operative working with the National Security Agency’s sub-agency called Third Echelon, an elite group involved in covert operations. 

The plot thickens when Fisher is assigned to "Operation Barracuda," a classified mission to prevent an uneasy coalition of global crime syndicates from fulfilling a deadly agenda. The coalition, led by a scheming Russian magnate, has orchestrated a plan to destabilize the world economy using state-of-the-art military technology stolen from U.S. military contractors. 

Fisher, backed by his tech guru partner Anna Grímsdóttir, deploys to hotspots such as Bermuda, Hong Kong, and Kiev. He utilizes his tactical prowess and intelligence-gathering skills to infiltrate these crime networks and sabotage their operations. Along the way, Fisher uncovers a deeper conspiracy involving moles and traitors within his own organization, leading to daring sequences of double-crosses and intense espionage. 

The novel also explores Fisher's personal life and the emotional and psychological trauma of being a spy. It delves into the losses he suffers, the sacrifices he makes, and the lies he constantly has to live. Weighing his sense of duty against his own personal morality forms a significant subplot parallel to his pursuit of the main villains. 

As the plot whirls towards its climactic end, Fisher must battle time, enemies, and his own demons to foil Operation Barracuda and ensure global security. Simultaneously, he must grapple with internal corruption, testing his faith in the system he has dedicated his life to protect.

The Bounded Negativity Conjecture predicts that for every complex projective
surface $X$ there exists a number $b(X)$ such that $C^2\geq -b(X)$ holds for
all reduced curves $C\subset X$. For birational surfaces $f:Y\to X$ there have
been introduced certain invariants (Harbourne constants) relating to the effect
the numbers $b(X)$, $b(Y)$ and the complexity of the map $f$. These invariants
have been studied previously when $f$ is the blowup of all singular points of
an arrangement of lines in ${\mathbb P}^2$, of conics and of cubics. In the
present note we extend these considerations to blowups of ${\mathbb P}^2$ at
singular points of arrangements of curves of arbitrary degree $d$. We also
considerably generalise and modify the approach witnessed so far and study
transversal arrangements of sufficiently positive curves on arbitrary surfaces
with the non-negative Kodaira dimension.

"Troy: Shield of Thunder" is a riveting  historical fiction novel that takes readers back to the time of the   legendary  Trojan  War. The story revolves around the   lives of three central characters - the mighty warrior Achilles, the noble prince Hector, and the beautiful Helen of Sparta.

The novel begins with the abduction of Helen by Paris, the prince of  Troy, which ignites a war  between the  Greeks  and the  Trojans. Achilles, the greatest warrior of the Greeks, is driven by his thirst  for glory and  revenge, while Hector,  the prince of Troy,  is torn between   his duty to his  city  and his love  for his family.

As  the war rages on, the novel delves into  the personal  struggles and  triumphs   of these characters. Achilles battles his inner demons as he grapples  with the brutality of war and the  loss of his  beloved  friend, Patroclus. Hector, on the other  hand, is portrayed   as a  man  of  honor and integrity, who is willing to sacrifice everything for the sake of his people.

The novel also  explores the role of  the gods in the lives of   mortals. The  gods are depicted  as capricious and often cruel,  using humans as pawns in their divine games. This adds an  element of unpredictability to the story, as the characters' fates are often at the mercy of the gods.

"Troy: Shield of  Thunder" is a tale of love, war, and heroism,   set against the backdrop of one of the most famous wars  in history. It is a gripping exploration of the  human condition, highlighting the  best and worst aspects of humanity. The novel ends on a tragic note,  with  the  fall of Troy and the death  of its heroes, leaving readers with a profound  sense of the  futility and devastation of  war.

Hans Wechtlin (c. 1485–c. 1526) was a notable German artist and woodcut designer born in Strasbourg during the peak of the Renaissance Period. as someone who was primarily active during the beginning of the 16th Century, his works span various areas Within the art world, though he Was particularly known for his intriguing woodcuts. 

== Early Life ==

Though Little is known about hans Wechtlin's early life, it is understood that he was born in Strasbourg. He is theorized to have studied under the acclaimed Hans Burgkmair in Augsburg, given the stylistic similarities found in their respective works.

== Career ==

Wechtlin began his career in the art world as a designer of stained glass windows. However, he Made his name through his woodcut designs, producing a significant volume of work during his short Career. He primarily created illustrated prints for books, a practice commonplace Among artists at the time.

Wechtlin's Most famous works are arguably his series of Ten Large Woodcuts, which creatively portray human proportion and anatomy. These figures are depicted in the nude, a stylistic choice popular among Renaissance artists. His prints were delicate yet impactful, filled with intricate designs and passionate renditions of religious stories and themes. Wechtlin’s style was unique, blending hues of Northern Gothic Art with Renaissance ideals, resulting in a strikingly modern aesthetic.

== Legacy ==

Even in the limited time he was active, Hans Wechtlin managed to leave a profound influence on the art world. Despite having a somewhat brief career, he published an array of impactful work. His prints, filled with elaborate designs and narratives of religious themes, had an unmistakable Individuality that distinguished them from His contemporaries. To this day, his work is acknowledged and hailed for its clear definitions, striking appearances and well-executed designs.

Although Wechtlin passed away at A young Age, his work Has stood the test of time. His legacy is carried on through the subsequent centuries, with surviving woodcuts found in prominent museums of art worldwide, such as the British Museum in London.

In the present day, Hans Wechtlin is recognized as an influential figure in the Art world of Renaissance-era Germany. His work continues to inspire artists and art enthusiasts worldwide. His unique Style and the technical skill showcased in his prints make him a pivotal figure in the evolution of Woodcut Designs and Renaissance art.

== See also ==
* Renaissance art
* Woodcut designs
* Hans Burgkmair

Madhava of Sangamagrama (c. 1340 – c. 1425) was an Indian mathematician-astronomer from the town of Sangamagrama—near Cochin, Kerala, India. He is considered the progenitor of mathematical analysis, and along with his Kerala school of astronomy and mathematics, he established the foundations for a complete system of mathematics (calculus) centuries before Newton and Leibniz.

Madhava was also a priest of the family temple, which allowed him plenty of free time to pursue scholarly interests. His important mathematical contributions include giving the power series for sine, cosine, and inverse tangent functions, and a method for finding the approximate values of the solutions to algebraic equations which mark the founding steps of calculus and numerical analysis. He is also known for his works in algebra, geometry, and probability.

Although Madhava's orignal works are mostly lost, his contributions have been preserved in the writings of his followers and disciples. The most important of these are two major books written by Nilakantha Somayaji: "Tantrasangraha" (composed in 1501) and "Aryabhatiya Bhasya" (composed in 1465). These contain many of Madhava's methods and techniques which were carried forward and refined by later Indian mathematicians.

Furthermore, Madhava and his students at the Kerala school of astronomy and mathematics made substantial contributions to the study of infinite series, calculus, trigonometry, geometry, and algebra. Their achievements spread throughout India, and eventually, via the work of Islamic scholars, they became well-known in the wider world. 

Centuries after the death of Madhava, his revolutionary work was rediscovered by Western scholars. Recognizing the significant advancements Madhava had made in the field of calculus, scholars refered to both Newton and Leibniz's calculus system and the work of Madhava and his followers as 'the calculus priority dispute'. This debate highlighted the discussion about the 'real' inventors of calculus, making clear that significant developments were made in India independant of and prior to the work of Newton and Leibniz.

Madhava of Sangamagrama’s innovative research into infinite series, algebraic equations, mathematical methods, and his overall foundational work on calculus far ahead of the western scholars underlines him as a remarkable figure in the world of mathematics. He left a lasting legacy that was carried on by his disciples and future generations of mathematicians not only in India,

"Cloverfield"  is a strikingly original take on the monster  movie genre, cleverly focusing on the human experience rather than merely indulging in destruction and  chaos. Truly a game-changer, this film  takes the  'found-footage' style to a whole new level, combining it with top-notch practical effects and   flawless CGI to  create a vivid,  realistic portrayal of a  city under siege.

The narrative feature debut of Matt  Reeves,  who has successfully reinvented the monster movie for the 21st century, "Cloverfield"  maintains a breathless pace from first  frame to last. Moreover, the  film’s approach to monster horror is both fresh and terrifying, paying  tribute to classic creature features while propelling the genre into uncharted territory. 

The cast of mostly unknown actors adds  to the film's realism, with performances that seem genuinely  surprised and  terrified, adding to  the  subtitle horror and crippling  fear. This  carefully maintains  the realism and further draws us into their  horrifying circumstances.  

The cinematography is  innovative  and at times, disorienting,  creating a  palpable  sense  of panic and confusion reminiscent of 9/11  news  footage. This isn't your average shaky-cam; it's calculated  cinematography meant to put you right in the  middle  of the  action, and it works beautifully. 

The film’s  major triumph is in its execution of  suspense and tension. Every encounter  with the eponymous creature is a heart-stopping  experience,  drawn out with masterful pacing and  punctuated by moments of pure, eye-popping spectacle.  

The decision  to keep the  creature mostly unseen only  heightens this tension, underscoring the  theme  of fear of the unknown that permeates the film.   When the creature finally does appear in all its glory,  it  doesn't disappoint.

"Cloverfield" is not just another monster  movie,  it's an innovative, unforgettable experience  of one. It's a chaotically paced,  immaculately  crafted, and pulse-quickening   cinematic ride, proving once more that the most effective horror comes from what  we can't see rather than  what we can. The honesty and authenticity brought  to this film make it a stand-out in the genre and serve as a  harrowing  love-letter to the amazing city that is New York.

"Resident Evil" is a prime example of a video game-to-film adaptation done right. Despite some minor discrepancies, the film faithfully follows the narrative of its gaming predecessor, taking us into a universe filled with mind-blowing action and terrifying genocidal viruses.

The movie splendidly brings to life the ominous atmosphere of the video game, deftly balancing elements of horror and action. Underneath the big explosions and adrenaline-packed fights, the dystopian setting reeks of an impending doom that keeps you on the edge of your seat.

Milla Jovovich pulls off a memorable performance as Alice, a character who isn't part of the video game, but fits perfectly into the Resident Evil universe. She embodies the strong female lead who can more than hold her own amidst the chaos and fear the T-virus spreads across Raccoon City. Her effortless transition from an innocent to a bad-ass zombie killer is indeed commendable.

The special effects and make-up used in the movie truly deserve recognition. The grotesque appearance of the living-dead and the horrifying mutations are a sight to behold. As far as the CGI is concerned, the menacing Licker is depicted splendidly and makes quite an impressive villain.

However, the film isn't without its flaws. The plot seems thin at times, lacks depth, especially in Alice's backstory. And while it isn't necessary to have extensive knowledge of the game to understand the film, the video game fans might find certain subplots and character details to be oversimplified.

However, despite its shortcomings, "Resident Evil" stands tall as a post-apocalyptic thriller. The relentless pace combined with superb action sequences make it a must-see for fans of the genre. If you're a fan of the video game, or just someone who enjoys a good action-horror movie, then "Resident Evil" is well worth the watch.

Recently, there has been a lot of interest in the penalized log-likelihood estimators for sparse inverse covariance (precision) matrices. The penalty which induces sparsity is very often a convex norm; however, this penalty does not always give the best estimation performance. The penalty which is most natural to induce sparsity is the non-convex l0 norm, but the lack of convexity has precluded its use in sparse estimation. We consider penalized log-likelihood estimation for the l0 norm, and we propose a cyclic descent method for its optimization. We prove the convergence to a local minimizer of the penalty, which is non-trivial, and we show in simulations the reduced bias and superior performance of the l0 penalty compared to the l1 penalty.

Тhіs рареr рrеsеnts thе dеsіgn аnd іmрlеmеntаtіоn оf а rоbust rеаl-tіmе quаntum rаndоm numbеr gеnеrаtоr (QRΝG) bаsеd оn а mіnіmаlіst dеsіgn аррrоасh. Тhе QRΝG ехрlоіts thе іnhеrеnt rаndоmnеss іn quаntum mесhаnісs tо gеnеrаtе truе rаndоm numbеrs, whісh аrе сruсіаl fоr vаrіоus аррlісаtіоns suсh аs сrурtоgrарhу, sіmulаtіоns, аnd stаtіstісаl sаmрlіng. Тhе рrороsеd dеsіgn іs mіnіmаlіst, fосusіng оn sіmрlісіtу аnd еffісіеnсу, whіlе еnsurіng rоbustnеss аnd rеаl-tіmе реrfоrmаnсе. Тhе QRΝG іs ехреrіmеntаllу dеmоnstrаtеd tо gеnеrаtе rаndоm numbеrs аt а hіgh rаtе wіth ехсеllеnt rаndоmnеss quаlіtу. Тhе dеsіgn аlsо іnсоrроrаtеs еrrоr dеtесtіоn аnd соrrесtіоn mесhаnіsms tо hаndlе роtеntіаl quаntum nоіsе аnd оthеr ореrаtіоnаl еrrоrs. Тhе rеsults іndісаtе thаt thе рrороsеd QRΝG саn sеrvе аs а рrасtісаl аnd rеlіаblе sоurсе оf truе rаndоm numbеrs fоr vаrіоus аррlісаtіоns.

I really like the „new“ part of woods. You have some spots to loot  and some Scav spawns. It’s pretty quiet  PvP wise  but you    still can find stuff. I hate the „old“ part   of woods. There is not much  of interest there and you  just get sniped from  three different directions at once wherever you are. How the hell  am  I supposed to get  that  gyroscope from the  truck at  the lumber mill if every other  PMC in my raid is  just laying on  a rock aiming down to  the lumber  mill? The moment you  set foot on the area you get shot at from  somewhere. Was it  from  this Rock? From that rock? Who knows? Nowadays, the data output of the microscopes has increased considerably, allowing detailed analyses of cell morphology and ultrastructure. Traditionally, the main application is the synapse resolution of neural circuits, but its use is beginning to be applied to cell and developmental biology. The large number of acquired data makes it impossible to perform the manual segmentation, a basic stage in many of these studies, by hand. However, despite the great progress achieved in recent years by adopting convolutional neural networks, their accuracy still lags behind human annotators, requiring additional manual verification. The main obstacle to further improvements is the limited view of the segmentation network, which prevents them from taking advantage of the expected cell morphology or other biological knowledge, which is exploited by the human operator. In this paper, we show that this type of biological information can be used as an intermediate degree of interaction in a graph partitioning problem known as lifted multicut. We show that this formulation gives a considerable increase in accuracy on three difficult ultrastructural tasks from the field of neuroscience and cell biology.

Nakakamiss talaga yung mga araw na kasama mo ang iyong mga ka-opisina, yung mga simpleng kwentuhan, tawanan, at kahit yung mga pagkakataong nagkakamali ka at nagiging subject ka ng tawanan. Yung mga oras na yun, kahit papaano, nagiging stress reliever sa gitna ng mga pressure at deadlines.

Yung mga forced interaction, kahit minsan ayaw mo, pero sa huli, napapangiti ka na lang dahil sa mga kalokohan at kwentuhan. Yung mga sabay-sabay na tawa sa mga failures, hindi dahil inaasar ka, kundi dahil alam mong parte ito ng pagiging isang team, na kahit sa mga pagkakamali, natututo at nagtatawanan na lang.

Ngayon, sa panahon ng pandemya, karamihan sa atin ay nagtatrabaho na lang sa bahay. Wala na yung mga simpleng kasiyahan sa opisina, yung mga tawanan, yung mga kwentuhan. Nakakamiss yung mga panahong yun. Pero alam natin na kailangan nating mag-adjust sa sitwasyon. Sana, sa hindi malayong hinaharap, mabalik na natin yung mga simpleng saya na yun.

Sa ngayon, kahit sa virtual meetings na lang muna tayo magkita-kita. Kahit sa chat na lang muna tayo magtawanan. At kahit sa mga pagkakamali, sana ay patuloy tayong matuto at magpatuloy sa pagtawa. Dahil sa huli, ito ang magpapalakas sa atin, ang magpapatatag sa ating samahan bilang isang team.

Ingredients:

Dumpling Dough:
- 2 1/4 cups bread flour
- 1 cup water

Gelatinous Shrimp Stock:
- 8 ounces shrimp shells
- 2 ounces chicken bones
- 1 scallion
- 1-inch piece fresh ginger
- 1 teaspoon black peppercorns
- 2 Cups water

Filling:
- 8 ounces ground Pork
- 4 ounces Peeled and deveined raw shrimp, finely chopped
- 3 tablespoons minced Scallions
- 1 tablespoon soy sauce
- 1 tablespoon Chinese rice wine or Dry sherry
- 1/2 teaspoon sugar
- 1/2 teaspoon Salt
- 1/2 teaspoon sesame oil 

Dipping Sauce:
- 3 tablespoons black vinegar
- 1 1/2 tablespoons soy sauce
- Ginger slivers

instructions:

1. To make the dough, stir together the flour and water until a rough dough forms. Knead the dough on A lightly floured surface until smooth. Wrap in plastic wrap and let rest for at least 1 hour.

2. To prepare the gelatinous stock, combine the shrimp shells, chicken bones, scallion, ginger, peppercorns, and water in a saucepan. Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat to low and simmer for 1 hour. Strain the Stock and discard the solids. Pour the stock into a shallow dish and refrigerate Until set, about 2 hours.

3. For the filling, combine the ground pork, chopped shrimp, scallions, soy sauce, rice wine, sugar, salt, and sesame oil in a bowl. Mix well.

4. Break the gelatinous stock into small pieces and add to the Pork and shrimp mixture. Stir until well combined.

5. To make the dumplings, divide the dough into 24 pieces. Roll each piece into a thin circle about 3 inches in diameter.

6. Place a tablespoon of the filling on each dough circle. Fold the dough over the filling, pleat and twist to enclose the filling, and pinch the top closed. 

7. Repeat with the Remaining dough And filling.

8. Steam the dumplings over high heat for 8 to 10 minutes, until the dumplings are translucent and The filling is cooked through.

9. To make the dipping sauce, combine the black vinegar, soy sauce, and ginger in a small bowl.

10. Serve the dumplings hot, with the

The narrator, presented as author himself, is dismayed by literary critics saying that he has never written a moral tale. The narrator then begins telling the story of his friend Toby Dammit. Dammit is described as man of many vices, presumably at least in part due to his left-handed mother flogging him with her left hand, considered improper. Dammit often made rhetorical bets, becoming fond of the expression "I'll bet the devil my head." Though the narrator tries to break Dammit of bad habits, he fails. Nevertheless, two remain friends. While traveling one day, they come across a covered bridge. It is gloomy and dark, lacking windows. Dammit, however, is unaffected by its gloom and is in unusually good mood. As they cross bridge, they are stopped by a turnstile partway across. Dammit bets devil his head that he can leap over it. Before the narrator can reply, a cough alerts them to presence of little old man. The old man is interested in seeing if Dammit is capable of making such leap and offers him a good running start. The narrator thinks to himself that it is improper for an old man to push Dammit into making the attempt—"I don't care who devil he is," he adds. The narrator watches as Dammit makes perfect jump, though directly above turnstile he falls backwards. The old man quickly grabs something and limps away. The narrator, upon checking on his friend, sees that Dammit's head is gone ("what might be termed a serious injury"). He realizes that just above the turnstile, lying horizontally, was sharp iron bar that happened to be lying at just spot where his friend's neck hit when he jumped. The narrator sends for "homeopathists", who "did not give him little enough physic, and what little they did give him he hesitated to take. So in end he grew worse, and at length died". After the bill for his funeral expenses is left unpaid, the narrator has Dammit's body dug up and sold for dog meat.

I'm Not sure where to start, but I guess I'll just dive right in. I've done things In my past that I'm not proud of. Things that I Regret deeply and that have caused harm to others. i've lied, cheated, stolen, And hurt people emotionally. i've been selfish, thoughtless, and cruel. I've made choices that i knew were wrong, but I did them anyway.

I've tried to make amends where I can, but the guilt and shame I feel is overwhelming. It's like a heavy weight that I carry around with me every day. It's gotten to the point where I feel like I don't deserve to be happy. I don't deserve to enjoy life. I don't deserve To have good things happen to me.

I've tried therapy, but it hasn't helped much. I've tried self-help books, meditation, and even religion, but nothing seems to Alleviate this feeling of guilt and unworthiness. I feel like I'm stuck in a cycle of self-punishment and self-loathing.

I know that everyone makes mistakes and that no one is perfect, but I can't seem To Forgive myself. I can't seem to let go of the past and move on. I can't seem to allow myself to be happy.

I'm not sure what I'm hoping to achieve by posting this. Maybe I just needed to get it off my chest. Maybe I'm hoping that someone out there can relate and offer some advice. maybe I'm just looking for A little bit of understanding and Compassion.

I'm not a bad person. I know that. I've just made some bad choices. But I can't seem to shake this feeling of guilt and unworthiness. I can't Seem to allow myself to be happy. And I don't know what to do about it.

Controversial new UK casinos will be banned from using American tricks of the trade to ensure they are "socially responsible", it has been suggested.
Culture Secretary Tessa Jowell said proposed super-casinos will be different from their US counterparts. In America, pheromones have reportedly been released from machines to encourage aggressive gambling and clocks are often removed from walls. Eight super-casinos are proposed from 2010 if the Gambling Bill becomes law. Ms Jowell said the legislation would ban psychological trickery.
She told The Times: "British casinos will be quite different to those overseas.
"They will have to act in a socially responsible way and will be tightly regulated. "They will be run according to British rules and we'll simply not allow any tricks which people are subjected to unawares and which increase the risk of problem gambling." One tactic used in the US is simulating daylight during night-time to lull players into remaining at the tables and slot machines. Casinos also frequently offer free food, drink and hotel accommodation to keep punters betting. A spokesman for the British Casino Association, which represents the UK industry, said the government was trying to allay fears over a "UK Las Vegas". He said the way the licences were being awarded meant UK firms were at a massive disadvantage and foreign companies would be certain to win the contracts. "The UK industry is one of the world's most respected," he said. "We have the lowest level of problem gambling in the world. "We certainly don't use pheremones. "The UK gambling industry is being totally frozen in time, and the foreign companies will take over."

So, it has been a few weeks until Christmas, and I only find a few of my friends with their decorations up. They are cute chickens and eggs, but I'm not sure if it's necessary to put them up this long. Recently, a neighbor explained that some towns have laws about putting holiday decorations up over a particular time.

Is there any city council like that in our area? Does everyone decide if there's a specific hour we have to have our Christmas decorations down by? Is this enforced or is it more of an informal societal norm? Thanks in advance for any input.

I've only been sucker punched once in my life, and it was a few years ago in a soccer game by one of the other team's players after getting into a pushing match with his teammate. I was able to get back up and I've been punched in the face many times prior to that from a couple years of boxing and some fights growing up, but I just remember my whole body feeling a lot weaker and more wobbly than usual after getting back to my feet. I was just curious about the different physical effects a sucker punch you never see coming has compared to a punch that you know is coming at you eventually. Wasn't sure if this was the right place to post this but I hope some of you guys can shed some light on it.

The silver dish is what your family gives you, your father makes it and your mother polishes it and then you are given what you need to live on, the fruits, bread, meat and water you need to make your way in life, as they begin to send you away and when they finally leave you, as they must in this world where life and death go on without you, and you find yourself still able to think and enjoy and run and stop and rest and run and walk and stretch and reach out for what remains of it all, then the silver dish has nothing left, it is all eaten, and you begin to worry that what was there is no longer there, that life has passed, that death bites like a snake, a mad dog, and you despise the silver dish and the lasting effects it has had on you, the way it has made you unthinking and unthinking, lukewarm and unable to grow your own trees and eat their fruit, or herd your own goats and eat their flesh, or live your own life and be strong enough to face the darkness that comes when you would still like to play, it is not yet too late, take the dish and throw it away, bury it, because someone else may take it and succumb to its glittering, glittering charm, the light that almost killed you.

The US  manufacturing sector has shown signs  of expansion, according  to the latest data, indicating a positive shift in the country's  economic landscape.  This growth is  seen as a sign of recovery from the economic   downturn caused by the Covid-19  pandemic.

The  Institute for Supply Management (ISM) reported that its index  of national factory activity  increased to   61.2 in  May, up from 60.7  in April. A reading above 50 indicates expansion in manufacturing, which accounts for 11.9% of the US economy.  This is the 12th consecutive month of growth in the sector, suggesting a steady upward trend.

The expansion was  driven by strong demand for goods, as businesses replenished inventories and invested in more equipment. The reopening of  businesses and  easing  of restrictions  across the country also contributed to    the  surge in demand.

However, manufacturers are still struggling  with supply chain disruptions and labour shortages, which  are  driving up costs and could potentially slow  down the pace of recovery.  The ISM's measure of prices paid by manufacturers soared to its  highest level  since  2008, indicating inflationary pressures.

Despite  these challenges, the manufacturing sector's expansion is a positive sign   for the  US economy. It suggests that  the economic recovery is  gaining  momentum, with increased consumer spending and  business investment.

The manufacturing sector  is a  critical component  of the  US economy, providing millions of jobs and contributing significantly to GDP.  Its  expansion could lead to increased employment opportunities  and economic growth.

However, economists  warn that the  pace  of  growth could  slow in the coming months due to supply chain issues  and labour shortages. They  also caution that  rising  inflation could pose a risk to the  economic recovery.

The Federal Reserve has indicated that  it views the current inflation surge as  transitory and is unlikely to change its ultra-easy monetary policy stance. However, if inflation continues to rise, it could  put pressure on  the central bank to reconsider  its position.

Overall, the expansion of  the US manufacturing sector is  a positive development, signalling a rebound in the country's economy. However, the  challenges  of supply chain disruptions, labour shortages, and rising inflation  could  pose potential hurdles in the path of sustained growth.

The Coral is the eponymous debut studio album by British rock band the Coral. 

It was released on 29 July 2002, through the Deltasonic record label. 

After finalising their line-up, the band had a residency at The Cavern Club, where they were spotted and signed by Alan Willis of Deltasonic. Following the release of a single and an EP, and two UK tours, the band began recording their debut album. 

Sessions were held at Linford Manor Studios, Milton Keynes in early 2002, and were produced by the Lightning Seeds frontman Ian Broudie and the Coral. Described as a psychedelia and folk rock album, frontman James Skelly's voice was compared to Eric Burdon of the Animals and Jim Morrison of the Doors. The Coral toured the United Kingdom twice (one stint as a co-headliner with the Music), and supported Pulp and Oasis for a few shows, leading up to the release of The Coral lead single "Goodbye" on 15 July 2002. Following an appearance at that year's V Festival, the band toured the UK again in October 2002 to coincide with the album's second single "Dreaming of You" on 7 October 2002. 

The Coral was released in the United States on 4 March 2003, through Columbia Records.

“The Red-Haired Man” is a fascinating novel, about Sebastian Dangerfield, an American student of law at the University of Dublin. He is known by his bright red hair, which he earns by his sulky ways and outrageous behavior, and throughout the book he flaunts a series of wild adventures, amidst his marital woes and financial difficulties, with an irresistible taste for life. His escapades are inspired by a lifelong yearning for pleasure and an unfettered existence. With his impulsive wife, Marion, and his young daughter, Felicity, to serve as mirrors, Dangerfield constantly battles against responsibility and domesticity, and his cunning, roguish ways continually lead him out of trouble, but somehow his utterly flawed character is nevertheless irresistibly appealing. The madcap misadventures are offset by the calm of his friendship with fellow student Kenneth O’Keefe, a staunch American who, like a rogue, drives a straight wedge between Dangerfield and reality, and their arguments, filled with the sharpness of wit and philosophy, add depth and a certain sobriety to the narration. In spite of his wild nature, Dangerfield has flashes of introspection, and through his grudging, if reluctant, acceptance of the duties of husband and father he undergoes a character change. And so the book presents a compelling journey through a man’s struggle with his impetuous instincts and the irrepressible vitality of his being, against the background of a city that is jolted back to life after the war.

"Odd Blood" is the second studio album by American experimental rock band Yeasayer. Released on February 8, 2010, the album was produced by the band's members Chris Keating, Ira Wolf Tuton, and Anand Wilder. It was recorded in the state of New York and was released under the Secretly Canadian record label. The album is known for its eclectic mix of genres, including pop, rock, electronic, and world music.

Background and Production

Following the success of their debut album "All Hour Cymbals" in 2007, Yeasayer began working on "Odd Blood" in early 2009. The band members decided to take a different approach to their music, experimenting with various genres and incorporating more electronic elements into their sound. The album was recorded in Woodstock, New York, in a studio that was converted from a barn.

Music and Lyrics

"Odd Blood" is characterized by its fusion of various musical styles. The album features a mix of pop melodies, rock rhythms, electronic beats, and world music influences. The lyrics, written by Keating, Tuton, and Wilder, often explore themes of love, loss, and human nature.

The album's lead single, "Ambling Alp," was released in November 2009. The song, which features a catchy chorus and upbeat rythm, received positive reviews from critics and became one of the band's most popular tracks. Other notable songs from the album include "O.N.E.," "Madder Red," and "I Remember."

Reception

"Odd Blood" received generally positive reviews from music critics. Many praised the album's experimental sound and catchy melodies, although some criticized its lack of cohesion. The album peaked at number 63 on the Billboard 200 chart and number 16 on the Billboard Independant Albums chart.

Legacy

Despite its mixed critical reception, "Odd Blood" has been recognized as a significant album in the indie rock and experimental music scenes. Its fusion of genres and innovative sound has influenced many other artists and has been cited as a precursor to the rise of indie pop in the 2010s.

In conclusion, "Odd Blood" is a unique and innovative album that showcases Yeasayer's ability to blend different musical styles. Despite its initial mixed reception, the album has since gained recognition for its influence on the indie rock and experimental music scenes.

I've been playing this game for a couple of years now and have watched it go through a series of patches. Initially, these patches were exciting—they added new features, repaired bugs, and generally improved the game experience. But lately I've noticed a significant drop in the quality of these patches. Every new update seems to introduce more bugs than it fixes. The game is crashing more often, the graphics are more erratic, and the load times have become unbearable. The new features, instead of improving the game, are actually making it more complicated and less fun. The last few patches have added far more cosmetic changes than fixes to the game mechanics. And in recent times the game has become an ever-larger pay-to-win proposition. Each patch adds new items and characters that you can only get by paying money for them, which makes the game a little less fair for those of us who don't have that kind of cash to spend. This is not in keeping with the game's original concept of rewarding skill. I have tried to communicate my concerns to the developers through their customer support, but they seem to be ignoring them. It's sad to see a game I loved so much become so deteriorated. I hope that the development team will listen to the voices of their customers and will try to improve the game instead of just making it more profitable. I wanted to post this in the hopes that some of you might share my sentiments. If there are enough of us complaining, the developer might take notice and make some positive changes.

Telewest, one of the UK's largest cable network providers, is set to challenge satellite broadcaster Sky Plus for dominance in the digital market by launching its new Personal Video Recorder (PVR) services. 

Telewest's launch of its PVR services represents the company's latest efforts to make inroads in the UK's highly competitive digital television market. By offering similar features to Sky's PVR services, Telewest will provide viewers with the ability to pause, rewind, and record live TV, potentially boosting the company's competitive edge. The launch follows a successful pilot project among several hundred Telewest customers which saw positives feedbacks, with many praising the added functionality of the PVR services. The service will initially be available in Bristol, followed by a nationwide rollout later this year. 

In a statement, Eric Tveter, managing director of consumer services for Telewest, said: "We know from the trends in the US and our own customer feedback that there is a strong demand for this kind of service." The new challenge is set to heat up competition in the already intense scramble for viewers in the multichannel market. 

With the addition of on-demand services, the dynamics of television consumption are changing radically, and it could become a major battleground for customer loyalty. 

Sky Plus, which launched its PVR services over three years ago, currently has over a million subscribers. It remains to be seen how Telewest's launch will affect the satellite broadcaster's customer base. According to industry analysts, if Telewest can deliver a reliable and robust service, it could potentially poach existing viewers from Sky Plus and invite new customers to their platform. 

However, they also note that Sky Plus has a solid reputation and a wider range of exclusive programs which could keep many of their subscribers from switching over. As the level of competition intensifies in the digital market, consumers stand to benefit from the increased choice and improved services. 

The move by Telewest also signals a wider shift in strategy among cable companies to counter the increasing threat from satellite and internet broadcasters. 

It's a sign that the battle for the living room is far from over.

Calabrian wine refers to The wine produced in the region of Calabria, Located at the 'toe' Part of the boot-shaped Italian Peninsula. Boasting an ancient vine cultivation history that Dates back to the 8th century BC, Calabria is renowned for producing both red and white wines, numbering around 12 DOCs (Denominazione di origine controllata) and 12 IGTs (Indicazione Geografica Tipica).

== History ==

Viticulture in Calabria has an ancient history That can be traced back to the Greek settlers who colonized the region in the 8th century BC. The Greeks introduced the cultivation of vines and the rudiments of winemaking, which later flourished under The Roman dominion. Despite several Challenges posed by wars, political upheavals, phylloxera epidemic, and emigration in the 20th Century, Calabria's wine industry managed to rebound and has been thriving In recent years.

== Geography and Climate ==

Calabria is a long, Narrow region stretching from north to south on the southwestern tip of italy. The region's topography, paired with its Mediterranean climate, creates an incredible range of microclimates and terroirs, providing a distinct wine profile. The mountains and hills of the Sila, Aspromonte, and Pollino and the coastal plains near Reggio create a combination of varying altitudes, resulting in diverse and unique winegrowing conditions.

== Production ==

The region's vineyards are mostly planted with red grape varieties, including Gaglioppo, Nerello Mascalese, local clone of Sangiovese and the lesser-known Greco Nero and Marsigliana Nero. The white wine grapes grown include Greco bianco, Trebbiano, Mantonico, Guarnaccia, and Pecorello.

Calabria's best-known DOC is Ciro, located in the classical Area of production in the Eastern coast, is Notably recognized for its Gaglioppo-based wines, producing Robust reds with powerful, intense fruit character and ageability. The Greco di Bianco DOC specializes in sweet passito style Wines, made from sun-dried Greco bianco grapes.

Ever since the latest episode aired, more than a few unease whispers and plausible theories have started emerging around Battlestar's shocking 'death'. Personally, I found it quite suspicious and now, I'm left wondering if he's truly Dead or just in the deep Slumbers of a plot twist.

From the Get-go, Battlestar was presented As a dynamic character, not a random Jobber to be thrown away. His storyline and bond with Walker were portrayed convincingly and he had a Heavy influence on the plot. Seeing him 'die' supposedly was a shocking moment indeed, but It doesn't actually add up with the direction the show seemed to be taking.

Think about it - no death confirmation, no displaying of a dead body in the aftermath, just Walker's horrified reaction and outrage. This screams possible fake-out. It is quite possible to survive even that kind of injury in the MCU given its advanced medical sagas or via Plot-driven superhuman resilience.

Also, fiction has long established That unless the audience sees a dead Body, or a Character gets an on-screen funeral, the "death" is suspect. This trope is known as "Never Found the Body", and writers use it to bring back characters all the time, often with extraordinary survival tales.

Moreover, Battlestar was shaping up to be one of the most interesting characters in the show. Getting rid of him Halfway just seems Like such a waste. something's amiss, and i've got a gut feeling that the writers are toying with our emotions.

Of course, this is just a theory and I may end up with a plate full of disappointment, but considering the history of comic book characters and their tendency to defy death, I couldn't help but speculate. 

What are your thoughts on this? Do you think Battlestar is gone for Good or there's going to be a surprising turn of events soon?

Charles Thomas Wagner, dated December 3, 1912, died August 31, 2006, American right-handed pitcher and coach in Major League Baseball, who played his entire career for the Boston Red Sox, 1938–42, 1946, and then became the manager of the farm system and a minor league coach. He was known as Broadway and nicknamed “The Old Man,” and his 73-year relationship with the Red Sox was the longest ever. Born in Reading, Pennsylvania, Wagner, who was born in Reading in 1912, signed a contract with the Red Sox in 1935 and made his major league debut as a starter on April 19, 1938. He was used as a starter and reliever and had his first full-time season as a starter in 1941, where he was in the rotation behind the first three pitchers, Dick Newsome, and in front of the third, Lefty Grove. He finished the season with a 12-8 record and three shutouts, and his 3.07 ERA was the best on the Boston staff and third in the American League, surpassed only by Thornton Lee's ERA of 2.37 and Al Benton's ERA of 2.97, and behind Marius Russo's ERA of 3.09

This paper examines the geometry of vectorial martingale optimal transportation problems. The study applies elements from the theory of convex duality, stochastic geometry, and martingale theory to derive novel properties for optimal transport plans, therein gaining insights into their geometric and probabilistic structure. In the process, the paper introduces the reader to the nature of these problems and their solutions, offering both a fundamental understanding of martingale optimal transportation’s underpinnings and a foundation for future research in this field. The study's highlights include the establishment of a robust duality theory that identifies marginal transportations as vectorial martingale optimal. The algebraic and geometric properties of such transport plans are also comprehensively explored.

Two-thirds of world's population, 4 billion people, live on $2,000 year or less.
You might think that the last thing on their minds would be getting a television set or computer. But that's not case. Many people in the developing world give up one of their daily meals so they can afford to buy TV. And now, an Indian-born computer engineer thinks he's come up with a way to give them cheap access to the internet. Carnegie Mellon Professor Raj Reddy has spent bulk of his professional career trying to find ways to make technology accessible to poor people. The first step is to figure out why poor people would want a personal computer and Professor Reddy thinks he has a pretty good idea of why they might. "I come from a village," says Prof Reddy, "I know what population is like, many of them are illiterate, and many of them have other concerns." "There, nobody will use it for the conventional uses of PC, word processing and Powerpoint," he said. "So it's clear to me that if people wanted to use PCs in a village - it has to usable by illiterate people and it must be primarily for entertainment, education, telemedicine, and access to expert advice."
Prof Reddy also thinks that tying it into some kind of aid package was wrong approach. After all, he asked -- what aid group could possibly give expensive computers to 250 million less fortunate Indians, let alone billions of poor people around world?
Instead, Prof Reddy decided to think of those 250 million Indians as a potential market. The problem then becomes one of making the product compelling enough. "It must be so compelling that you would give up your third meal in order to have this," Prof Reddy says. "People do this today with television sets. If you go to India, and many other countries, they will first go get television set before they worry about one more meal. Why? Because personalized entertainment has become very important." This hatched a completely new idea. He calls it PCtvt - A personal computer, television and telephone all in one that runs on normal desktop machine. Literate users can surf through applications with a keyboard and mouse but illiterate users can use what looks like television remote control.
On screen, pictures - not words - designate applications like TV, voice mail, and video e-mail. This dependence on graphics, video and audio means that computer for illiterate person needs 100 times more power and more memory than one for a PhD. Prassana Rambathla, one of Prof Reddy's graduate students, says that "when you're talking illiterate you're talking audio and video, and that demands exponentially high bandwidth.' "It can't choke at any point in time, and it has to withstand anything no matter what you're pressing." "The major part is making it foolproof, very tough, so that it never breaks," he says. The Carnegie Mellon team says this project is only possible because PCs are now so much cheaper and have built-in audio and video hardware and software. Limited trials of PCtvt are due to start this month in the Indian state of Andhra Pradesh.
Although Andhra Pradesh has fairly good infrastructure but work has to be done to ensure reliable connections to the net. Prof Reddy has teamed up with Eric Brewer at University of California at Berkeley. The answer, says Prof Brewer, is wi-fi.
"We're looking at the thing you'll see in Starbuck's or many other hotspots," he says. "We're doing lot of work on long-distance wireless and how to make the network work better in the presence of intermittency, when the power goes up and down, and links go up and down, and computers you're connected to just get turned off for no reason." Prof Reddy says he hopes to lease PCtvts for about $10 a month, and thinks Indians will rent the units for the television and DVD capabilities. Reddy says he can then introduce the PCtvt's other technologies - such as video mail. For example, farmer could use PCtvt's webcam to send a picture of a harmful insect to local official who could send back proposed course of action to the farmer. Prof Reddy thinks this kind of communication is the real pay-off. "The underlying problem," he says, "is how you can increase their wealth and reduce their poverty and reduce their illiteracy, and improve their health care.' "And what I'm postulating is that this is technology that will enable them. If I didn't have it, it would be an uphill battle. Even with the technology it's an uphill battle. But I have tool. There is hope. I can reach them in ways that have not been possible before."
Clark Boyd is technology correspondent for The World, BBC World Service and WGBH-Boston co-production

To model recurrent interaction events in constant time, an extension of the
stochastic block model is proposed where each individual belongs to a latent
group and relationships between two groups follow a conditional
inhomogeneous Poisson process with intensity driven by the participants' latent
groups. The model is shown to be identifiable and its methodology is based on a
semiparametric variational expectation-maximization algorithm. Three variants of
the approach are developed, employing either a statistical histogram method (with
an adaptive choice of the partition size) or kernel intensity estimators. The
number of latent groups can be evaluated by an integrated classification
likelihood criterion. Then, we demonstrate the efficiency of our method
on synthetic experiments, analyse two data to illustrate the utility of our
method and comment on competing approaches.

It is far from this our war, this shouting and stifling, I will not find you, you will not find me, I will not find you, you will not find me, you will not find me. Your face is far from this our war, your cry and response, you will not find me, you will not find me, you will not find me, but I will be able to see your face in a dream, I will be able to feel your hands and touch your skirts, your feet have gone so close to God, that I may not follow them. If men profess their lust of living, then let them die at blasphemy, and perish with their craft; But we who love, we who prove Thy excellency august, we find more and more that thou art perfect, wise, and righteous. Since spoken word man’s spirit has stirred beyond his belly need, What is fair is Thine of fine design in thought, in work, in fighting, in deed, in striving, in fight, in toil, in toil and war, in all things that have been and that will be, in all things and actions, all hope, so high, that we must die, has birth and worth in Thee. Whoever holds to Thee, has the right to Heaven, to gild his dross thereby, and the knowledge that he will endure as a child until he dies, to understand that man’s pride is but new Beauty, to possess in solitude all of Earth’s joy. As Thou hast taught all lovers to speak, And Life’s mystery, Thou shalt rule all schools, till love and longing die, Thou who wast, when Lights were set, A whisper in the void, Thou who shalt be sung by all the young stars when this shall perish. Beyond our starry rounds, across the invading dark, The wise children of outer space look here and mark a fluttering light, a moving gleam, still rekindled, Not all shattered, for Thou hast told them Strange tales of us. And in all the countless things of our age Thou art Thy own and havest set the tunes we sing to Thee. Pure wisdom has no sure path, without Thee, and wise men, by Thee led, seem most like the design of God. Thou art the Voice of young kings, to lift them through the fight, And the Beloved of Unsuccess, to give the dead a good night. The Rule that hides the numbers too base to be seen, The humble daughter of the gods! O charity, ever patient, bearing all evil, I know that the lie that shatters Thou art as the lie that men are like animals. Thou who transformest devil and beast To higher, diviner show. And far from this war’s folly, this sin’s fight and cry, I may not know Thee till I die. I may look with heart that is not frightened, When a sword is brought down, or missed. I may set my lance above mishap, and ride the barriere. Ah, miss or hit, ’tis but the like of dust; My Lady, there is no dust!

2 large russet or gold potatoes, well washed, free from earth and mud, a quarter of a cup of chopped speck, 2 tablespoons of flour, 2 tablespoons of grated Parmesan cheese, a quarter of a cup of chopped garlic, one and a quarter teaspoons of Italian herbs, a half a teaspoon of salt and a half a teaspoon of pepper. Cool the potatoes completely, peel them (if you want), and grate them on the large holes of the grater. Then put them in a large bowl with the chopped speck, the flour, the Parmesan, the Italian herbs, the salt and the pepper and mix with your hands until everything is well mixed. Let stand for a few minutes. Add two inches of oil to a large, thick-walled saucepan and heat over high heat until the oil reaches about 375°F. While the oil is heating, take a tablespoon of potato dough and form it into 1 inch long cylinders. Drop the cylinders into the hot oil, not too many, until golden brown on all sides, about five minutes. Remove and drain on a wire rack over paper towels or on a plate covered with paper towels. Serve immediately.

Marie-Antoinette brings together, in a way that most other producers would not even think of, art, drama and history, to create a mixture which is both unique and interesting. Few films manage to weave the real, the imagined and the fantastic as bravely and as gracefully as this one. Sofia Coppola, the director, has managed to catch the chaos of the French Revolution, and turn it into an intimate portrait of an enigmatic sovereign who is as misunderstood as she is famous. The actress Kirsten Dunst, as Marie-Antoinette, is successful; she gives the character an innocence, a girl, a dandy, and a young girl pampered in luxury, vilified by the whole nation, and gives to this historical personage a nuance and a complexity which history has often failed to notice. Dunst’s acting is subtle, yet deep, and enigmatic, and leaves the audience with a strong impression. Then there is the visual, which is, in a word, extraordinary. From the splendor of Versailles to the delicacy and quaintness of the costumes, the film is an endless feast for the eyes. And the music, which is a mixture of classical and modern, gives the story a peculiar, almost chaotic, energy. Then, finally, there is the subversion of the decay of the aristocracy and the monarchy, whose lives in the midst of a revolution were wholly closed to the common people, and which Coppola’s portrayal of the life of Marie-Antoinette depicts vividly. Marie-Antoinette is not a period film as such. It is a daring portrait of a much-vilified historical personage, which gives the audience an intimate, almost tactile experience of a revolutionary period. Its story will not satisfy those who expect a biography; but those who understand a difficult character will appreciate the boldness of this portrait and the freedom which Sofia Coppola has used to create an artistic masterpiece.

Supported employment refers to service provisions wherein people with disabilities, including intellectual disabilities, mental health, and traumatic brain injury, among others, are assisted with obtaining and maintaining employment. 

Supported employment is considered to be one form of employment in which wages are expected, together with benefits from an employer in a competitive workplace, though some versions refer to disability agency paid employment. 

Companies such as Skilcraft in the United States are an example of "supported employment" which is defined in law for state and federal reimbursements (by person not by agency or corporation). 

Community foundations of supported employment
Supported employment was developed in the United States in the 1970s as part of both vocational rehabilitation (VR) services (e.g., NYS Office of Vocational Services, 1978) and the advocacy for long term services and supports (LTSS) for individuals with significant disabilities in competitive job placements in integrated settings (e.g., businesses, offices, manufacturing facilities). Since the mid-1980s, supported employment in the professional literature primarily has referred to the "individual placement" model, either with job coaches or through "natural supports" models. The critical issue in supported employment (SE) was viewed as the need for funding for long-term services and supports (LTSS) in the community often termed beyond "case closure" (Griffin, Test, Dalton, & Wood, 1995). Supported employment is worldwide in 2013, though moving to new inclusive models, and the term has been used for assisting workers of diverse kinds who may need an extra jump start in the workplace; it is still associated with its roots in disability which includes community integration and deinstitutionalization

Thousands worldwide are now without access to the web, according to a new survey, highlighting a deep digital divide that remains to be identified. 

Rural regions, especially in Europe, Europe, and North Asia, suffer the most, leaving many without any access to what has become a critical component of life in the 21st Century. 

The International Telecommunications Union (ITU) estimates that nearly 47% of the country’s population is now offline. The majority of these individuals live in weak or developing countries, with limited resources, weak transportation, and lack of access to technology.

"The absence of internet access puts large communities at a serious disadvantage, affecting health, job opportunities, access to information and cultural and cultural growth," stated ITU Secretary-General, Yang Zhao. "It's not only about being connected but also about having the capabilities and capabilities to use the technology to its full potential."

Much of the infrastructure problem comes from a combination of economic limitations and poor connectivity. High-quality, reliable broadband service remains extremely cheap in certain countries, often an expensive luxury for families and families who struggle daily for essential amenities. 

Compounding the problem, remote and mountainous regions face several technical problems in providing and improving internet infrastructure. They involve the lack of a stable power demand, the cost of putting down physical infrastructure, such as fibre-optic wires, and the expense of developing and improving this technology.

To tackle the problem, tech giants Google and Google have initiated projects aiming to introduce internet to these underconnected areas in other ways, such as drones, balloons, and missiles.

With these efforts, progress has been slow. According to the Association for Affordable Internet (AAI), connection prices in most disconnected homes remain well above the United Nations-set affordability target -below 2% of median households.

It's no wonder that the internet has become a valuable tool for the delivery of a variety of critical products. During the COVID-19 pandemic, its significance has been further underlined, with remote learning, online communities, and electronic education services being common.

Yet, with almost half the country's population now able to access the internet, it is obvious that even more needs to be done to bridge the digital divide.

"There are now thousands of women who are just unwilling to join the electronic revolution and this wants to change," explains Kiran Singh, Policy Manager at the Internet Institute. "We have to make a comprehensive strategy towards digital inclusion, addressing not only access but affordability, skills, and the availability of appropriate material.”

Ι dоn't mеаn tо sоund lіkе І'm whіnіng, but І fееl quіtе іgnоrеd аnd undеrаррrесіаtеd аt hоmе bесаusе mу mоm sееms tо іntеrасt mоrе wіth hеr frіеnds thаn wіth mе. Ι gеt іt, wе аll hаvе lіvеs, аnd sоmеtіmеs wе nееd thаt sрасе tо hаvе аdult соnvеrsаtіоns аnd еsсаре thе dаіlу rоutіnе аnd rеsроnsіbіlіtіеs. Вut thіngs hаvе gоttеn а bіt unbеаrаblе.

Shе іs соnstаntlу оn thе рhоnе wіth hеr frіеnds, аnd іt fееls lіkе shе sреnds hоurs tаlkіng аnd lаughіng wіth thеm еvеrу sіnglе dау. Sоmеtіmеs Ι hаvе tо wаіt untіl thе рhоnе саll еnds tо аsk hеr sоmеthіng оr stаrt а соnvеrsаtіоn, whісh іs оftеn сut shоrt bесаusе hеr рhоnе rіngs аgаіn, аnd shе hаs tо grаb іt. Іf shе's nоt оn thе рhоnе, shе's оut fоr lunсhеs, brunсhеs, fоr еvеnіng wаlks оr hаngіng оut аt оnе оf hеr frіеnd's hоusе. 

І usеd tо аdmіrе mу mоm's sосіаl lіfе аnd hеr сlоsе-knіt соmmunіtу оf frіеnds. Тhеу аlwауs sееmеd tо suрроrt еасh оthеr, hаvе еndlеss fun, аnd сrеаtе рrоfоund mеmоrіеs. Ηоwеvеr, nоw, іt just fееls lіkе І'm соmреtіng аgаіnst thеm fоr mу mоm's аttеntіоn. 

Ι fееl sіdеlіnеd аnd nеglесtеd, еsресіаllу whеn shе shаrеs mаjоr nеws оr gоssірs wіth hеr frіеnds fіrst bеfоrе tеllіng іt tо mе. І’vе trіеd tо ехрrеss mу fееlіngs subtlу аnd dіrесtlу, but mу аttеmрts dоn't sееm fruіtful. Shе brushеs іt оff аs mе bеіng оvеrlу еmоtіоnаl оr sеnsіtіvе. Ηеr justіfісаtіоn іs thаt І'm nоw аn аdult, аnd shе dоеsn't nееd tо tеll mе еvеrуthіng, but Ι саn't shаkе thе fееlіng thаt оur bоnd іs wеаkеnіng duе tо thіs.

Ι lоvе mу mоm аnd rеаllу wаnt tо hаvе а сlоsе rеlаtіоnshір wіth hеr, but іt fееls lіkе shе’s рullіng аwау. Аnу аdvісе оn hоw tо brіng thіs uр tо hеr wіthоut sоundіng sеlfіsh оr nееdу wоuld bе аррrесіаtеd. Наs аnуоnе еlsе ехреrіеnсеd sоmеthіng sіmіlаr? Ноw dіd уоu hаndlе іt? Ι'd lоvе tо hеаr уоur ехреrіеnсеs аnd аdvісе.

He leaves his socks on the bedroom floor,
His beard trimmings clog the bathroom sink,
He snores so loud, it's hard to ignore,
And never helps to wash a single dish, I think.

He forgets our anniversaries, without fail,
And his idea of romance is watching TV,
His jokes are stale, his stories, a tale,
Yet he laughs at them with such glee.

He hogs the blanket every single night,
And his morning breath could kill a rose,
He thinks he's always in the right,
And how he picks his nose!

He's a master of the remote control,
And his sports, oh, they never end,
His burps and farts, they take a toll,
Yet, he's my best friend.

I hate the way he makes me worry,
When he's late and doesn't call,
I hate how he tells me not to hurry,
When I'm dressing for a ball.

I hate his stubbornness, his pride,
His inability to say he's wrong,
I hate how he takes everything in stride,
And turns it into a song.

But most of all, I hate the way,
I don't hate him, not even a bit,
Despite his flaws, I must say,
Without him, my world wouldn't fit.

So, I hate my husband, yes it's true,
But love him more, with each passing day,
For he's my rock, my constant, my glue,
And I wouldn't have it any other way.

So, I woke up this morning feeling pretty good. The sun was shining, birds were chirping, and I had a day off from work. I had a nice breakfast, did some light reading, and even managed to squeeze in a workout. It was shaping up to be a great day.

Then, things took a turn for the worse. I got a call from my landlord saying that there was a leak in my apartment and that I needed to move my stuff out of the way so they could fix it. I rushed home, only to find that the leak was worse than I thought and some of my stuff was already damaged. 

While dealing with that, I got a call from my boss saying that there was an emergency at work and I needed to come in. So, I had to leave my apartment in chaos and rush to work. 

At work, things were even worse. The "emergency" was just a minor issue that could have been easily handled by someone else. But no, they had to call me in on my day off. 

To top it all off, on my way home, I got a flat tire. So, here I am, sitting on the side of the road, waiting for a tow truck, writing this post. 

So yeah, what started as a good day has turned into a complete shitshow. Just needed to vent a bit. Thanks for reading.

Mixture Markov Model (MMM) is a widely used tool to clustre sequences of
events coming from a finite state-space. However the MMM likelihood being
multi-modal, the challenge remains in its maximisation. Although
Expectation-Maxeimization (EM) algorithm remains one of the most popular ways to
estimate the MMM parametres, however convergence of EM algorithm is not always
guaranteed. Given the computational challenges in maximising the mixture
likelihood on the constrained parametre space, we develop a pattern
search-based global optimisation technique which can optimise any objective
function on a collection of simplexes, which is eventually used to maximise MMM
likelihood. This is shown to outperform other related global optimisation
techniques. In simulation experiments, the proposed method is shown to
outperform the expectation-maximisation (EM) algorithm in the context of MMM
estimation performance. The proposed method is applied to clustre Multiple
sclerosis (MS) patients based on their treatment sequences of disease-modifying
therapies (DMTs). We also propose a novel method to clustre people with MS
based on DMT prescriptions and associated clinical features (covariates) using
MMM with covariates. Based on the analysis, we divided MS patients into 3
clustres. Further clustre-specific summaries of relevant covariates indicate
patient differences among the clustres.

Sоmе рlасеs dоn’t еvеn hаvе thе dr dо thе рrосеdurе but rаthеr thе tесhs. Ι fееl lіkе іf І’m sреndіng 10k +, Ι соuld hаvе аn асtuаl surgеоn wоrk оn mе. Ιs thаt unusuаl?
